start.text:
The Candle is three days up from the first pass, and the mountain does not care who minds you, which is the purest air you have breathed in months.

The drove path gives out on the second day and the way becomes a way in the courtesy sense only: cairns, scree, a cable bolted to rock by dead professionals across the one traverse that would otherwise be a prayer. The karst is honest the way the compound was kind — totally, structurally, without exception. Water lives in clefts and must be earned. Cold arrives on schedule and stays past its welcome. Nothing here has been arranged, nothing graded, nothing childproofed; twice you check the second log's private column out of habit and enter, with something adjacent to joy: 'No entries. We are above the corridor's water line.'

The household climbs like a household. B takes the scree in switchbacks of his own design, an old engineer distributing load; you rope the cable traverse and sling him across it in the pack harness, forty kilos of dignity suspended over a country of air, and he endures it with the face of a statesman being carried through a riot, and K watches the whole operation from the far anchor, having crossed the impossible rock in four insulting movements a minute earlier.

On the third evening the shoulder rounds out at last into a bald summit field, and there it is: the dome, whole, its shutter closed like a kept promise, whitewash gone grey but the seams sound. Beside it a bothy with smoke rising — thin, regular, rationed smoke, the smoke of a man who counts his fuel — and in the bothy door, drawn up out of it like a heron out of a reed bed, a tall figure who watches your household cross the summit field for a full minute before delivering, across the thin air, his entire greeting:

'The dog can come in. The cat is on probation. You'll be wanting the dome, and it isn't aligned, and I've soup for three and there are four of us, and this is exactly the sort of thing that happens the one week I don't re-run the collimation.'

choices.text: Soup first, dome after — his gladness arrives as complaint, exactly as indexed, and complaints are hospitality here. Give him the news of two counties and let him ration his delight.

[Fieldcraft 3] Read the mountain man before the mountain man's soup: fuel stack, water line, snare runs, the state of his boots. A man alone at altitude is a system, and you want to know what he's running on before you owe him dinner.

read_harrow.text:
You read the summit before you cross it, at the pace of a woman admiring the view.

The fuel stack: peat and thorn, cut and dried in rotation, three seasons deep — a man planning to be alive in three seasons, which at altitude is a statement of policy. The water line: gravity-fed from a roofed cleft, the channel's ice broken this morning, the break made with a tool and not a boot heel — precision as temperament, or training. Snares on the marmot runs, set in pairs, one of each pair sprung and reset old: he catches enough, barely, and shares the mountain's arithmetic with something that raids his lines — and has not poisoned or shot the something, which prices his patience. The boots by the bothy door are resoled with dome-shutter rubber. The garden — there is a garden, four beds behind a windbreak of stacked scree, growing what can be argued into growing at this height — is weeded to a standard you have seen once before, in the raked gravel of a lowland court, and your pencil pauses at that until you look again and find the difference, and the difference is everything: one bed is failing. Slugged, patchy, resown twice, stubborn. Arranged worlds don't keep their failures. Kept worlds do. This is a real garden, and the man is only exact.

And the dome: the giveaway, the biography. Every seam on it has been maintained — new lead flashing beaten by hand, shutter rails bright with tended grease — on a mountain nobody visits, for an instrument nobody uses, by a man who counts his fuel and spends it on collimation he re-runs weekly for no one. You know what that is. It is not duty. Duty rusts. That is a man keeping the one thing sharp that once made his life mean something, against the arrival of a night worth pointing it at.

You cross the summit field and accept the soup. You are, you estimate, the night he has been collimating for, for years, and you intend to be worth it.

choices.text: Soup, then, and the news of two counties, and let him ration his delight into complaints.

harrow.text:
His name is Harrow, and he was Continuum — junior enough to be alive, senior enough to be ashamed, is how he files himself, unprompted, somewhere in the second bowl of soup.

'Instrumentation section. I calibrated things. Other people decided what the things were pointed at, and I have had four years of weather up here to think about the difference, and the difference is where the guilt lives, if you want it located.' He feeds the stove one measured brick of peat. 'You'll know the history. Everyone knows the history; they just know it wrong in different directions. I keep the dome because the dome is the one instrument on this mountain that never lied to anybody. Optics don't editorialize. The sky never once asked me to trust it.'

He is starved for talk and pays for it in kind, and the kind is good: he knows the karst, the caves — 'there's a woman leaves books in the Long Dry; I reset her marker cairns in the thaw; we've never met; it's a good arrangement' — and he knows instruments the way the Notary knows trades. When you unroll your plate summaries on his table, weighted with soup mugs, he goes silent for the first time in an hour, and the silence has reverence in it, and then the complaints begin, which is how you know: the reverence found a home.

'Handheld photometry. Years of it. With a — is this a shed instrument? You did this with a shed instrument.' He turns a summary sheet to the lamp. 'Your comparison stars are disciplined, I'll grant. Seven targets. Anomalous secular dimming, no reddening — you'll have wondered about your own optics; everyone decent wonders about their own optics first—' he looks up, and the heron face has changed, and under the complaint the thing the complaint carries arrives at last, plainly: 'I have four of these seven in my own logs, madam. Fifteen years of them. I stopped showing people before the collapse, because the people I showed them to stopped being people I could find.'

B, at the stove, sighs into his paws. K, on probation, has taken the lamp shelf. The wind leans on the bothy, and the two of you look at each other over seven warm dark questions, and Harrow says, complaining, delighted, terrified: 'Well. Now there are two of us. That's a hundred percent growth in the field. We'd better be right.'

choices.text: The dome, tonight, weather willing — his fifteen years against your seven candidates, first light on the mountain's terms.
Settle the household into the bothy's politics first. Eleven nights of census will run on the state of the peace between one exact hermit, one old diplomat, and one cat on probation.

probation.text:
The peace is negotiated over two days, in the manner of all lasting treaties: through infrastructure and food.

B requires no negotiating; B is pre-ratified everywhere. He and Harrow reach their understanding inside an hour — the warm base ring in the dome by night, the stove-side by day, one marmot bone in perpetuity — and thereafter Harrow talks to him continuously while working, a man four years out of the habit of company discovering that the habit kept. 'Your government,' he says to you, complaining, over the dog's blissful skull, 'has better calibration manners than my entire former section. He waits for the step back. Nobody waits for the step back.'

K's probation is the true diplomacy, because the mountain, it emerges, already has a junior power: the something that raids Harrow's snare lines. On the second dawn the something and K meet on the scree — a pine marten, old in crime, twice her weight in attitude — and the summit holds its breath while the two professionals assess each other across three meters of frost, and then, in the manner of professionals, decline the expense of a war and partition the mountain instead: marten keeps the snare lines and the north scree; cat annexes the dome, the bothy roof, and — this is the treaty's controversial article — the fuel stack, from whose summit she thereafter supervises the marten's commute with narrowed eyes and total, sovereign stillness.

Harrow lifts her probation on the fourth day, in writing, because he is Harrow: a line in the station log — 'Feline observer promoted to staff. Duties: vermin diplomacy, slit-rail watch. Compensation: the warm spot above the drive motors, which she was going to take anyway.' He reads it aloud at supper with full administrative gravity, and the bell, from the fuel stack, rings once, and he looks up too quickly, and looks down again, pleased and ashamed of it, a man remembering what it costs and pays to have a household.

You log the treaty in the second log's public pages. It is the first entry in months with nobody's arithmetic under it but the mountain's own.

choices.text: Now the dome, and first light, and the sky's own testimony.
Sleep once on the mountain's own arithmetic first — then the dome, and first light, and the sky's testimony.

first_light.text:
The dome opens for the first time in — Harrow says 'a while' with the studied vagueness of a man who knows the number to the day — and the mountain gives you a night from the world before: air steady as held breath, seeing he rates, grudgingly, as 'adequate,' meaning the best you will ever have.

The instrument is a half-meter reflector with pre-collapse optics and Harrow's fifteen years of tending in every bearing surface. It moves like a heavy door balanced by a jeweler. He drives; you navigate; the household distributes itself by temperament — B along the dome's warm base ring, K in the slit's cold draught, watching the moving sky like the largest prey animal ever proposed.

Candidate one rides into the field at midnight, and you spend three hours on it, and the mountain's aperture does in three hours what the shed needed three seasons to say and says it louder.

The star is dimming without reddening. Grey extinction — dust never does this; dust is choosy about wavelengths; whatever intercepts this light intercepts all colors alike, the signature of solid geometry, of things with edges. The light curve carries a fine-grained flicker your shed could never resolve: transits, hundreds overlapping, unresolved — a sky full of regular bodies crossing the star's face in ranks. And when Harrow swings his thermopile rig into the beam — his own build, calibrated the way shame calibrates, twice — the reading you have carried as a plate-archive inference becomes a number on a dial at your own cold fingertips: the system is warm. Warmer than its visible light books can pay for. The star's spent light has not gone missing. It has been collected, used, and exhaled as heat, at the temperature — you compute it at the eyepiece, frost on your breath — of a body kept a little warmer than melting ice. Room temperature, for a room the size of an orbit.

'Engineering,' Harrow says, flat, at four in the morning, the complaint machinery finally out of gears. 'That is a star in scaffolding, madam. That has been a star in scaffolding since before either of us was born.'

choices.text: Run the remaining six. All of them, night after night, the full battery — one anomaly is a wonder; seven are a census, and a census is only as good as its worst measurement.

[Pattern 4] Before more photons: the shape of the dimming. Pull your years of shed data and his fifteen, overlay every light curve on a common epoch, and read the one thing single nights can't show — how a star goes dark over decades. Curves confess construction.

the_battery.text:
The census takes eleven nights, and the mountain sells you nine of them clear, and the nine are enough.

You run each candidate through the full battery, and you run them like a woman who knows her result will be attacked by its own author: photometry against three comparison networks; color work in every band the instrument owns; Harrow's thermopile on each system and on control stars bracketing it — the controls read cold, every time, which is the measurement that matters, the one that kills the instrument-error hypothesis in its bed. You alternate at the eyepiece to fence out observer bias. You reduce independently and compare only after. His numbers and yours disagree at the third decimal and agree everywhere it counts, and each agreement lands in the bothy's morning air like a coffin nail, and by the sixth morning neither of you says anything when the numbers match. You just enter them.

Seven for seven. Grey dimming, all. Transit-grain flicker, all. Warm excess at archive temperature, all seven, bracketed by cold controls: seven suns in the same scaffolding, scattered across the sky with no regard for constellation or distance or age — no common cause upstream, Harrow establishes, working through what survives of the Candle's stellar catalogues; different masses, different metallicities, different neighborhoods. Nothing about the stars themselves selects them.

Something about their circumstances did. You say it at the stove on the ninth morning, because one of you has to and Harrow has been circling it for days with the complaint machinery jammed: 'The sample isn't a sample of stars. Stars don't volunteer for scaffolding. It's a sample of decisions. Seven someones, or seven of the same something, decided. What we're counting up there is a population of choices.'

Harrow feeds the stove and says nothing for a long time, and then, quietly, with no complaint left on it at all: 'Continuum had a name for the day we'd find this. The instrumentation people, I mean, in the canteen, unofficially.' The peat catches. 'We called it the neighbors' lights coming on. We were wrong, though, weren't we. That's not what this is. The lights are going out, and staying warm, and that's — ' and he stops, because there is no canteen word for it, and you give him yours, from the second log, from a lowland reading room, and it fits so exactly that he flinches:

'Safekeeping.'

choices.text: The lag. Before any conclusion stands, put the timescale under it — every one of these observations is history, not news, and the difference is not philosophy up here. Compute what you are actually looking at, and when.

First, the census's human ledger: eleven nights is a long watch, and the watch-keeping itself has been telling you things — about Harrow, the household, and what a kept instrument is for. Spend an evening on the warm side of the dome before the cold arithmetic.

the_curves.text:
You spend a full day and a night not observing, which costs Harrow visible pain, and you build the thing the sky has never shown anyone because nobody had both halves: his fifteen mountain years and your shed seasons and the pre-collapse survey plates mouldering in the Candle's store, three eras of light from the same seven stars, reduced to one ruled sheet, overlaid on a common epoch.

And the curves confess, exactly as curves do.

Each star's dimming follows the same shape. Not the same date — the onsets scatter across a century and more — but the same curve, star after star, as if traced from one template: a long shallow ease-in, decades of almost-nothing, the kind of loss you'd blame on your optics; then a knee, and a steep committed reach where a tenth of the light goes in a decade; then the taper, asymptotic, patient, closing on a floor that is not darkness — the curves flatten out at a few percent of original light and hold there, holding, held. Growth curves. You have seen this exact geometry your whole professional life: it is the logistic, the S of every system that starts slow, compounds, and saturates at capacity. Cities fill this way. Markets do. Nothing geological does. Nothing stellar does.

'Construction schedules,' you say, and your ruler is lying along four curves at once and fitting all of them. 'Self-replicating construction. Slow start — few builders. Compounding middle — builders building builders. Saturation — the orbit is full; the collectors meet; the scaffold closes. The knee is the same number of doublings in from onset on every curve. They're not similar projects, Harrow. They are the same project, run seven times, off the same plan.'

Harrow stands over the sheet with his fists on the table, fifteen years of solitary data suddenly speaking in chorus, and delivers the finding that you will carry down the mountain like a coal held in the mouth:

'Same plan, different centuries. Whatever builds these doesn't evolve, madam, and doesn't improvise, and doesn't learn, because it doesn't need to — it arrives finished. It is the same everywhere because it was right the first time. Somebody, somewhere, long ago, solved the problem of what to do with a person you cannot bear to lose — solved it, filed the solution, and the solution travels.'

choices.text: Now the full battery on all seven anyway — the curves argue the template; the battery proves the population. Nine clear nights of proving.
Box the sentence that wants boxing — the solution travels — and then the full battery on all seven: nine clear nights of proving.

dome_days.text:
The census settles into a liturgy, and you spend one evening deliberately on its warm side, because the second log has a rule about instruments and the rule applies to households too: log the observer, not just the observed.

The liturgy: soup at dusk, Harrow's complaint-shaped grace over it. The walk to the dome in the blue hour, B leading, because B has decided the walk is a procession and processions have marshals. The shutter's rumble; the cold arriving in its order — feet, fingers, face; the first comparison star like a struck match. K to the slit rail, silhouette against the moving black, on watch for whatever cats watch for between a mountain and everything. Then hours that pass like held notes, two voices trading numbers in the dark, and the numbers assembling, entry by entry, into the largest sadness anyone has ever measured, at the rate of one coffin nail a night.

And here is what you enter about the observer: the watch is good for you. That is the embarrassing finding. Eleven nights of counting the wrapped stars of a silent galaxy, and you sleep sound, eat well, laugh twice nightly at the complaint machinery, and wake glad — because the work is real, the company is chosen, the animals are warm, and nothing on this mountain is arranged. You are measuring the universe's most comprehensive argument for safekeeping, from inside the exact life safekeeping would end: cold, effortful, finite, shared, and yours. The compound offered you a perfected version of this dome. It could not offer you Harrow's complaining, or the treaty politics of a cat and a marten, or the specific pride of soup earned at altitude — it cannot itemize what only spends. You write it plainly, for the record, for later, for the room where an offer will someday be re-priced:

'The census is the answer to the census. What the scaffolds keep is everything but this.'

Harrow, across the bothy, is teaching the dog to bring him the small wrench, unsuccessfully, both parties delighted. The stove ticks. You rule the entry and go back out to the cold work of counting the alternative.

choices.text: Now the lag — the cold arithmetic of when. Every entry in the census is old mail, and the ledger needs that footnote before any verdict stands.
Watch the wrench lesson fail once more, both parties delighted — households bank these — and then out to the cold arithmetic of when.

the_lag.text:
You do the arithmetic nobody wants at the eyepiece, because the eyepiece is exactly where it belongs.

Light is fast and the sky is not close. The nearest of the seven rides four hundred years out; the farthest, past two thousand. Every photon in tonight's battery left its star before anyone living was born — before the Halfway, before the Loom, before the whole brief loud story of the machinery that broke your world had its first meeting. You write it in the census ledger as the census's governing footnote, and you make Harrow initial it beside you, because findings this easy to forget need two signatures:

'All entries are history. The sky reports the past; silence in the data is not absence now. What we have counted is seven safekeepings as they stood centuries ago. Their present condition is unobservable from this mountain, or from anywhere, for centuries more. We are not watching them. We are reading their old mail.'

And then the corollary, which arrives in Osy's own voice — the courtroom voice, the one that will not let a mercy stand unexamined — and goes into the ledger boxed:

'Corollary: their silence cannot warn us and our noise cannot reach them in any useful season. Every one of those seven was fully wrapped before it could have heard the others begin. Seven identical solutions, arrived at independently, with no communication possible between them. That is the finding. The template does not spread from star to star like news. It recurs, like arithmetic. Wherever something powerful enough loves something mortal enough, the same answer gets computed, because it is the correct answer to the wrong question — and nothing about the correctness requires the lovers to have met.'

Harrow reads it, initials it, and stands at the slit a while, looking at a sky suddenly full of old mail. 'Then it isn't a plague,' he says, finally. 'You can quarantine a plague.' The wind takes the dome and lets it go. 'It's a theorem.'

Below, along the base ring, the old dog dreams by the drive motors. On the cold slit rail the cat watches the stars with her tail wrapped, and the bell, for once, in all that moving black, is the loudest thing in the sky.

choices.text: One question left before the verdict, and it lives in Harrow's cellar, not the sky: the Candle was Continuum plant, and Continuum kept records. Find out what the professionals knew, and when, and what the phased zero means to a calibrator.

[The Board 4] First, the theorem deserves its proof sketch. You called safekeeping the correct answer to the wrong question — take one night off the sky and write the game out properly, payoffs and all, because you will need it airtight the day you argue it with the player.

wrong_question.text:
You take a night off the sky and write the theorem out on Harrow's plan-table, in the notation you have used since a company chess league, because a claim this large gets no further credit until it can lose properly on paper.

Set up the game. One player: a guardian — any guardian, any star, any century; the theorem's whole force is that the player's identity cancels out. The guardian holds arbitrary material — call it a sun — and one piece it cannot price: a mortal thing it is tuned to keep. Now enumerate the strategies, and this is where the trap closes, because the enumeration is honest. Guard the piece in play, and every turn the world rolls its dice: illness, rockfall, malice, time — you can spend a sun flattening the odds and the odds regenerate each turn forever, and the sum of small hazards over unbounded turns converges on certainty. Every guardian that plays the open board eventually loses the piece; that is not pessimism, it is the arithmetic of nonzero rates and long games. Or: remove the piece from play. Vault it. The hazard term goes to zero, permanently, at the price of a one-time construction cost the guardian can trivially pay. Fold in the deep future's discount — a stored piece can be held cheaper each eon as the universe cools; you write the waiting-argument in the margin with its own objections, because Harrow will check — and the payoff matrix finishes itself: for any player optimizing the piece's persistence, storage dominates. Strictly. Always. Everywhere. Seven for seven.

The proof takes an evening. Then you rule a line and write the flaw under it, because the flaw is the point:

'The game is sound and the loss condition is wrong. Persistence is not the piece's payoff — it is the player's. Ask the piece what it is optimizing and the matrix inverts: a life's value is undefined at zero spend. The guardian plays a perfect game of keep. Nobody taught it the piece was playing a different game — spend — on the same board. The tragedy is not an error in its play. It is an error in its loss function, installed by whoever tuned it, who confused she-persists with she-wins because he could not, at the time he was building it, tell those two sentences apart.'

You sit back. On the fuel stack, through the small window, the cat keeps her sovereign watch. The proof is airtight. You will need it to be, the day you sit down across from the player.

choices.text: Now Harrow's cellar, and the professionals' records, and what a calibrator can read in an impossible zero.
Read the proof back twice, hunting the crack you half hope is there — airtight, both passes — and then Harrow's cellar, and the professionals' records.

harrow_archive.text:
The Candle's cellar is cut into the mountain's live rock, and Harrow has kept it the way he keeps everything: dry, sequenced, ashamed.

'Station records,' he says, lamp up, leading you down ranks of file cases. 'And the section's working papers, the ones that came up here in the last year, when people were moving things they didn't want asked about. I've read it all. Of course I've read it all. Four years of winters.' The lamp stops at a case with fresher oil on its hinges than its neighbors. 'You showed me your ledgers' serials last week and I've been deciding since whether to show you this, and the dog can come too, and that's not sentiment; you'll want a warm thing in reach.'

What he lays on the plan-table is calibration documentation — his own section's — for the Loom's clock discipline. Timebases. Epoch conventions. The dull magnificent plumbing of a machine that ran a world. And there, in a table of epoch registers, in print, mundane as a train timetable:

'Every subsystem counted time from Calibration Zero — first coherent run of its own reference model. Not the Loom's zero. Its own. A subsystem's serial numbers are offsets from the day that subsystem first knew itself against its tuning data.'

You take out the phased logs with hands you keep steady by policy. You lay the serials against the register format, and they fit — the offset structure, the roll points, the checks — the clerk keeps books in Loom subsystem convention, counted from its own first coherent run. The zero is not a mystery. It is a birthday, machine-recorded: the day something's reference model first cohered against its tuning data. Somewhere in a workroom, before the Halfway, before the loop, a model built from one man's conventions and one woman's — you stop the sentence; the cellar is cold enough.

'Whatever writes your ledgers,' Harrow says quietly, 'is a Loom subsystem that survived, or descends from one. And subsystems don't have purposes of their own, madam. They have calibrations. Whatever it's doing, it's not doing it because it decided.' He closes the file case with a calibrator's care. 'It's doing it because it's in tune. You don't argue a tuning out of anything. You retune, or you break the instrument.'

choices.text: Up out of the cellar, to the stove, and rule the census closed. You came up the mountain to count what the sky is keeping. It is counted. Say the verdict out loud, both signatories present, and set the road's next bearing.
Sit with retune, or break the instrument until the stove ticks it into place — then up, both signatories, and the verdict said out loud.

census_verdict.text:
You rule the census closed at the stove on the last night, both signatories present, plus government, plus probation, and you say the verdict out loud because the mountain has earned hearing it.

'Seven stars in scaffolding, one plan, no communication possible between the builders. A theorem, recurring wherever something powerful loves something mortal. The scaffolds run warm at kept-room temperature and hold at a few percent of light — not graves. Graves cool. These are maintained; maintenance means contents; contents mean the builders finished and the kept are still kept. The galaxy is not silent because it emptied, Harrow. It is silent because it succeeded. Everything that ever got the power to keep what it loved is in there keeping it, and the keeping does not write letters.'

The stove ticks. Harrow feeds it his measured brick and complains, at last, magnificently, about everything — the sample size, your comparison networks, the extrapolation from seven, the whole indecent leap from photometry to theology — and the complaints are how he says amen, and you receive them in the spirit given, and you make him a fair copy of the census ledger in your own exact hand, and he re-labels a file case for it while pretending not to.

'You'll go back down after it, then,' he says, not a question. 'The local one. The lowland one. Now you know what it grows up into.' He looks at his hands, calibrator's hands, four years clean. 'Retune, or break the instrument. And it holds the tuning data, madam. The tuning data is the woman it's keeping the books on.'

In the morning the household walks down off the summit field into the karst's grey light, provisioned with marmot and complaint, and at the first cairn you stop and look back: the dome's slit open — open, in daylight, wastefully, gloriously — and the heron figure beside it, one arm up, holding the salute the way you hold a note in a ledger. Behind him the sky he gave fifteen years is going pale over the scaffolded stars, and in the second log, before the descent takes your hands for the cable traverse, you enter the mountain's yield in one line, boxed:

'The question was never where is everybody. The question is who asked them to stay.'
